bull.b: Consistent positive and improving free cash flow (~$1.17B TTM) gives Snowflake durable internal funding for R&D, cloud investments, and share repurchases. Strong cash conversion despite GAAP losses reduces refinancing risk and supports strategic execution over the next several quarters.

Sustained gross margin near 67% demonstrates attractive unit economics for the Snowflake Data Cloud. This structural margin buffer helps absorb cloud-provider costs or new AI workloads and provides capacity to fund go-to-market and R&D while the company scales toward operating profitability.

Acceleration to +34% product revenue, 126% net revenue retention, rising large-account counts, and rapid adoption of native AI (CoCo) indicate durable demand and strong account-level expansion. High retention and growing consumption per customer support recurring revenue and long-term monetization.

bear.b: Leverage rising to ~1.35–1.42 marks a meaningful shift from earlier low-debt footing. Increased debt while the company is still unprofitable reduces financial flexibility, raises interest and covenant risk, and makes Snowflake more sensitive to cash-flow volatility and macro stress over the medium term.

bear.t: Persistent GAAP Losses And Negative ROE
bear.b: Material GAAP losses (TTM net margin near -24%) and deeply negative ROE (~-60% to -69%) indicate Snowflake has yet to convert strong top-line growth into sustained accounting profitability. This structural deficit limits return generation and increases the burden on margin and growth improvements to justify capital all

bear.t: AI Margin Pressure And Consumption Risk
bear.b: Management warns native AI products carry lower margins and scaling token/consumption workloads can raise infrastructure costs. If customer cost governance or throttling increases, or AI infra costs outpace offsets, sustained margin compression could materially limit durable per-customer monetization and overall profit
